source_paper_title: Online Bipartite Matching with Reusable Resources
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Delong_2024, title={Online Bipartite Matching with Reusable Resources}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0242}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0242}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Delong, Steven and Farhadi, Alireza and Niazadeh, Rad and Sivan, Balasubramanian and Udwani, Rajan}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1825–1854} }
context:
Page 27, Section 5 (Conclusion), paragraph titled “problems”

The main problem left unanswered in our paper is whether one can obtain the competitive ratio of \(1 - 1/e\) for OBMRR.
title: Achieve the optimal 1 minus 1 over e competitive ratio in reusable-resource matching
background_latex:
Online bipartite matching with reusable resources (OBMRR) is defined on a bipartite graph \(G=(V,U;E)\), where \(V\) is a set of offline vertices (reusable resources) and \(U\) is a sequence of online vertices (requests) arriving over discrete times \(j=1,2,\dots,|U|\) in an oblivious adversarial order. When request \(j\) arrives, its incident edges \(\{(i,j)\in E: i\in V\}\) are revealed, and an online algorithm must either match \(j\) to an available neighbor \(i\in V\) or leave \(j\) unmatched; decisions are irrevocable.

Reusability is governed by a known deterministic usage duration \(d\in\mathbb{N}\): if resource \(i\) is matched at time \(j\), then \(i\) becomes unavailable for the next \(d-1\) arrivals and becomes available again at time \(j+d\). Equivalently, each resource \(i\) can be matched at most once in any window of \(d\) consecutive times.

In the vertex-weighted version, each resource \(i\in V\) has a nonnegative weight \(r_i\). Each time \(i\) is matched, the algorithm earns reward \(r_i\). The total reward of an algorithm is the sum of rewards over all matches it makes.

Let \(\mathrm{ALG}(G,d)\) denote the (expected) total reward obtained by a (possibly randomized) online algorithm on instance \((G,d)\), where the expectation is over the algorithm's internal randomness. Let \(\mathrm{OPT}(G,d)\) denote the maximum total reward achievable by an offline algorithm with full knowledge of the entire arrival sequence and graph, subject to the same reusability constraints.

The competitive ratio of an online algorithm is the largest \(\Gamma\in[0,1]\) such that for all instances \((G,d)\), \(\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{ALG}(G,d)]\ge \Gamma\,\mathrm{OPT}(G,d)\). For the classic non-reusable online bipartite matching (obtained as a special case when \(d=|U|\)), the optimal competitive ratio is \(1-1/e\) for randomized algorithms, and this value also serves as an upper bound for OBMRR because OBMRR generalizes the non-reusable model (for large \(d\), it reduces to the classic case).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether there exists a randomized online algorithm for vertex-weighted OBMRR with deterministic usage duration \(d\) that achieves competitive ratio \(\Gamma = 1-1/e\). That is, does there exist an online algorithm such that for every instance \((G=(V,U;E),d,\{r_i\}_{i\in V})\),
\[
\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{ALG}(G,d)] \;\ge\; (1-1/e)\,\mathrm{OPT}(G,d)?
\]
If not, determine the optimal achievable competitive ratio for OBMRR.
latex:
Online bipartite matching with reusable resources (OBMRR) is defined on a bipartite graph \(G=(V,U;E)\), where \(V\) is a set of offline vertices (reusable resources) and \(U\) is a sequence of online vertices (requests) arriving over discrete times \(j=1,2,\dots,|U|\) in an oblivious adversarial order. When request \(j\) arrives, its incident edges \(\{(i,j)\in E: i\in V\}\) are revealed, and an online algorithm must either match \(j\) to an available neighbor \(i\in V\) or leave \(j\) unmatched; decisions are irrevocable.

Reusability is governed by a known deterministic usage duration \(d\in\mathbb{N}\): if resource \(i\) is matched at time \(j\), then \(i\) becomes unavailable for the next \(d-1\) arrivals and becomes available again at time \(j+d\). Equivalently, each resource \(i\) can be matched at most once in any window of \(d\) consecutive times.

In the vertex-weighted version, each resource \(i\in V\) has a nonnegative weight \(r_i\). Each time \(i\) is matched, the algorithm earns reward \(r_i\). The total reward of an algorithm is the sum of rewards over all matches it makes.

Let \(\mathrm{ALG}(G,d)\) denote the (expected) total reward obtained by a (possibly randomized) online algorithm on instance \((G,d)\), where the expectation is over the algorithm's internal randomness. Let \(\mathrm{OPT}(G,d)\) denote the maximum total reward achievable by an offline algorithm with full knowledge of the entire arrival sequence and graph, subject to the same reusability constraints.

The competitive ratio of an online algorithm is the largest \(\Gamma\in[0,1]\) such that for all instances \((G,d)\), \(\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{ALG}(G,d)]\ge \Gamma\,\mathrm{OPT}(G,d)\). For the classic non-reusable online bipartite matching (obtained as a special case when \(d=|U|\)), the optimal competitive ratio is \(1-1/e\) for randomized algorithms, and this value also serves as an upper bound for OBMRR because OBMRR generalizes the non-reusable model (for large \(d\), it reduces to the classic case).

Determine whether there exists a randomized online algorithm for vertex-weighted OBMRR with deterministic usage duration \(d\) that achieves competitive ratio \(\Gamma = 1-1/e\). That is, does there exist an online algorithm such that for every instance \((G=(V,U;E),d,\{r_i\}_{i\in V})\),
\[
\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{ALG}(G,d)] \;\ge\; (1-1/e)\,\mathrm{OPT}(G,d)?
\]
If not, determine the optimal achievable competitive ratio for OBMRR.
open_problem_quote: The main problem left unanswered in our paper is whether one can obtain the competitive ratio of \(1 - 1/e\) for OBMRR.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally search for hard instances (graphs and arrival orders) and estimate the empirical competitive ratio of candidate algorithms via simulation, but certifying an upper bound against all algorithms or proving tightness is not directly amenable to finite search. Still, automated discovery may uncover candidate lower-bound instances that refute specific algorithmic approaches toward \(1-1/e\).
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate online policies can be parameterized (e.g., priority scores, reranking schedules, correlated selection rules) and evaluated on generated adversarial or worst-case-seeking instance distributions using a computable fitness signal such as estimated worst-case competitive ratio over a test suite. An evolutionary loop could iteratively improve algorithm designs or produce informative adversarial families that challenge \(1-1/e\) aspirations.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In Delong et al.'s OBMRR model, each offline vertex (resource) can be matched at most once in any length-\(d\) window, and the objective is vertex-weighted reward \(\sum r_i\) over matches. The paper highlights that the natural LP relaxation is not integral (integrality gap at least \(7/6\) for \(d=3\)), so achieving the classic \(1-1/e\) ratio for integral algorithms is nontrivial even though the fractional/large-capacity regime admits \(1-1/e\) via balance-style methods. Their own contributions (Periodic Reranking at \(\approx 0.589\) and an OCR-based primal-dual algorithm at \(\approx 0.505\)) establish the first guarantees beating the \(1/2\) greedy barrier for unit-inventory reusable matching, but still fall short of \(1-1/e\).

Forward-citing work has largely reinforced \(1-1/e\) as the right asymptotic benchmark while leaving the exact unit-capacity question open. The strongest progress toward \(1-1/e\) comes from large-inventory/large-capacity analyses: "Asymptotically optimal competitive ratio for online allocation of reusable resources" proves realization-independent algorithms with competitive ratio \((1-1/e-\delta)\) where \(\delta\to 0\) as \(c_{\min}\to\infty\), and also shows an asymptotic \(1-1/e\) upper bound, establishing optimality in that limit. Related large-inventory robustness results (Batched Inventory Balancing under inventory shocks) similarly obtain ratios converging to \(1-1/e\) as initial inventory grows, even with adversarial replenishments.

On the algorithmic-tools side, work on k-rental problems develops lossless dependent online rounding for fixed deterministic durations, suggesting a potential pathway: if one can design a strong fractional OBMRR policy (perhaps via the OBMRR LP or a tighter relaxation) whose marginals satisfy appropriate online-rounding conditions, then one might lift near-\(1-1/e\) fractional performance to the integral setting. However, current rounding results either apply to identical-unit feasibility systems (k-rental) or to non-reusable matching (lossless online rounding for classic OBM), and do not yet yield a constant \(1-1/e\) guarantee under the bipartite-compatibility plus sliding-window reuse constraints. Overall, the problem of achieving an exact \(1-1/e\) competitive ratio for vertex-weighted OBMRR with deterministic duration \(d\) and unit inventory remains open; the best known worst-case ratios for integral algorithms in this model remain below \(1-1/e\), while \(1-1/e\) is achievable only asymptotically in large-capacity regimes.

sources.title: Asymptotically optimal competitive ratio for online allocation of reusable resources
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper gives a randomized online algorithm for online bipartite matching/allocation with reusable resources and stochastic usage durations that is asymptotically optimal in the large-capacity regime: for OBMR with capacities \,\(c_i\) and known usage distributions \,\(F_i\), there exists a realization-independent algorithm (Sample G-ALG) with competitive ratio \((1-1/e-\delta)\) where \(\delta=O(\sqrt{\log c_{\min}/c_{\min}})\) and \(c_{\min}=\min_i c_i\); hence the ratio tends to \(1-1/e\) as \(c_{\min}\to\infty\). It also proves an upper bound that no online algorithm can beat \(1-1/e\) asymptotically. Additionally, a simpler Rank-Based Allocation (RBA) policy is shown \((1-1/e-\delta)\)-competitive for several distribution families (e.g., IFR, exponential, certain two-point).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether vertex-weighted OBMRR with deterministic usage duration \(d\) admits a randomized \((1-1/e)\)-competitive algorithm for all instances (no large-capacity assumption). This paper achieves \((1-1/e)\) only asymptotically as capacities \(c_i\) grow, and primarily for stochastic durations; deterministic duration is a special case, but the guarantee still relies on the large-inventory regime (and/or distributional assumptions for the simpler RBA). Thus it does not resolve the open problem in full generality (notably, it does not give a \((1-1/e)\) guarantee for unit capacities / general capacities, which corresponds to the original OBMRR model with one reusable unit per resource). It does, however, establish optimality of \(1-1/e\) as an asymptotic limit and provides algorithmic/analytic tools (LP-free certificate, fluid-guided rounding) relevant to pursuing the full result.

sources.title: Robustness of online inventory balancing to inventory shocks
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper studies online assortment/allocation with reusable inventory (deterministic return durations \(d_i\)) and adversarial nonnegative exogenous replenishments. It proposes Batched Inventory Balancing (BIB) and proves (Theorem 1) that for any concave nondecreasing penalty \(\Psi\) and batch threshold \(\gamma\in[1,c_0]\), BIB is \(\Gamma(\Psi,\gamma)\)-competitive against a clairvoyant benchmark, where \(\Gamma(\Psi,\gamma)=\min\{\Gamma_1(\Psi,\gamma),\Gamma_2(\Psi,\gamma)\}\) with
- \(\Gamma_1(\Psi,\gamma)=\min_{x\in[0,1-1/c_0]}\Big\{1-\frac{x}{1/c_0+(1+\gamma/c_0)(1-\Psi(x))+\int_{x+1/c_0}^1\Psi(y)\,dy}\Big\}\) (as stated in the paper),
- \(\Gamma_2(\Psi,\gamma)=\min_{x\in[0,1-1/\gamma]}\Big\{1-\frac{x}{1/\gamma+1-\Psi(x)+\int_{x+1/\gamma}^1\Psi(y)\,dy}\Big\}\).
In particular, choosing the exponential penalty \(\Psi(x)=\frac{e^{1-x}-e}{1-e}\) and \(\gamma=\Theta(\sqrt{c_0})\) yields a competitive ratio that converges to \(1-1/e\) as the minimum initial inventory \(c_0\to\infty\) (large-inventory/asymptotic regime), even with both endogenous (returns after \(d_i\)) and exogenous shocks.
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether vertex-weighted OBMRR with deterministic usage duration \(d\) admits a randomized online algorithm achieving the optimal \(1-1/e\) competitive ratio for all instances (no large-inventory assumption). This paper does not resolve that in full generality. Instead, it proves an asymptotically optimal \(1-1/e\) guarantee in a different but related model (online assortment/allocation) with reusable resources and even additional exogenous replenishments, under a large-inventory regime (competitive ratio approaches \(1-1/e\) as \(c_0\to\infty\)). For OBMRR with capacity 1 per offline vertex (the usual matching setting), \(c_0=1\), so the theorem does not yield \(1-1/e\) and does not close the gap. Nonetheless, the algorithmic idea (batching units) and the randomized primal-dual/interval-assignment analysis are relevant tools that might be adaptable to OBMRR or to the multi-unit (\(b\)-matching) generalization of OBMRR.

sources.title: Leveraging reusability: Improved competitive ratio of greedy for reusable resources
sources.theorem_or_result:
In online weighted bipartite matching with reusable resources and random i.i.d. usage durations per resource, the Greedy policy is provably competitive against an optimal clairvoyant benchmark (knows the request sequence but not duration realizations):
- If each resource returns in one step with probability at least \(p=\min_i \Pr(D_i=1)\), then \(\mathrm{Greedy}/\mathrm{OPT}\ge 1/(2-p)\).
- If usage durations are geometric \(D_i\sim\mathrm{Geom}(p_i)\) with \(p=\min_i p_i\), then \(\mathrm{Greedy}/\mathrm{OPT}\ge (1+p)/2\), and this bound is tight (approachable on a 2-step, 2-resource instance).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether vertex-weighted OBMRR with deterministic usage duration \(d\) admits a randomized online algorithm achieving the optimal \(1-1/e\) competitive ratio (vs the offline optimum under the same deterministic reusability constraint). This paper does not address the deterministic-duration model nor the \(1-1/e\) target. Instead it studies a different OBMRR variant with random usage durations and analyzes the (deterministic) Greedy algorithm against a weaker benchmark (a clairvoyant that knows arrivals but not duration realizations). It shows competitive ratios improving with a reusability parameter \(p\), but the guarantees are of the form \(1/(2-p)\) or \((1+p)/2\), which do not imply \(1-1/e\) in general and are not stated against the full offline optimum for deterministic \(d\). The coupling/lost-match analysis technique may be a related tool for analyzing reusable-resource matching, but it does not resolve the \(1-1/e\) question for deterministic \(d\).

sources.title: Online Rounding and Pricing Schemes for k-Rental Problems
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies adversarial online allocation with reusable identical units (k-rental). For kRental-Fixed (all rentals have the same deterministic duration d), it gives a relax-and-round algorithm (dop-\(\phi\)-fixed) that computes an online fractional solution via a price function \(\phi\) and then rounds it using a new dependent rounding subroutine (Algorithm 2) that is lossless: for any feasible fractional targets \(\hat x_n\) satisfying \(\hat x_n \le \min\{1,\, k-\sum_{j<n}\hat x_j\mathbf 1\{a_j+d>a_n\}\}\), the rounding allocates with probability exactly \(\hat x_n\) (Theorem 1). Under bounded valuations \(v_n\in[v_{\min},v_{\max}]\), dop-\(\phi\)-fixed achieves competitive ratio \(1+\ln(v_{\max}/v_{\min})\) with \(\phi(y)=v_{\min}\exp((1+\ln(v_{\max}/v_{\min}))y-1)\) (Theorem 2), and no online algorithm can do better than \(1+\ln(v_{\max}/v_{\min})\) in that model (Proposition 3).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether vertex-weighted OBMRR with fixed deterministic reuse duration \(d\) admits a \((1-1/e)\)-competitive randomized online algorithm (matching the classic non-reusable optimum) or, failing that, what the optimal ratio is. This paper does not resolve that: it analyzes a different but related model (k identical reusable units with per-request valuations, not a general bipartite graph with heterogeneous vertex weights and adjacency constraints). Its main relevance is methodological: it introduces a lossless online dependent rounding scheme (1-ocr) for fixed-duration reusability, which could be a component in designing improved OBMRR algorithms if one can obtain strong fractional guarantees for the matching LP. However, the competitive ratios proved here for kRental-Fixed are of the form \(1/(1+\ln(v_{\max}/v_{\min}))\) (in their convention) and depend on bounded valuation ranges, rather than achieving a constant \(1-1/e\) independent of value spread. Thus it provides partial progress/related tools but not a \((1-1/e)\) algorithm for vertex-weighted OBMRR.

sources.title: Online Rounding Schemes for -Rental Problems
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies adversarial online allocation with reusable identical units (k-rental). For kRental-Fixed (all rentals have the same deterministic duration \(d\)), it gives a relax-and-round algorithm (Algorithm 3) that computes an online fractional solution via a pricing function \(\phi\) and then rounds it using a new lossless online rounding scheme (Algorithm 2). The rounding result is: Algorithm 2 is a \(1\)-ocr (Theorem 1), i.e., for any feasible fractional targets \(\hat x_n\) satisfying \(\hat x_n \le \min\{1,\, k-\sum_{j<n}\hat x_j\mathbf 1\{a_j+d>a_n\}\}\), it produces integral allocations with \(\Pr[x_n=1]\ge \hat x_n\) for all \(n\). For kRental-Fixed under bounded valuations \(v_n\in[v_{\min},v_{\max}]\), their pricing+rounding algorithm dop-\(\phi\)-fixed is \((1+\ln(v_{\max}/v_{\min}))\)-competitive (Theorem 2), and no online algorithm can beat this ratio in that model (Proposition 3).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether vertex-weighted online bipartite matching with reusable resources and fixed deterministic reuse time \(d\) admits a \((1-1/e)\)-competitive randomized algorithm (matching the classic non-reusable optimum). This paper does not address that question in the general OBMRR model: it studies a different special case with \(k\) identical resources (no bipartite graph structure/compatibility constraints) and request-specific valuations, plus an additional bounded-valuation assumption \([v_{\min},v_{\max}]\). Its main relevance is methodological: it introduces a lossless online rounding scheme (1-ocr) for fixed-duration reusability that could potentially be useful as a rounding component in OBMRR algorithms. However, its competitive-ratio results are logarithmic in \(v_{\max}/v_{\min}\), not the constant \(1-1/e\), and it does not prove any \(1-1/e\) guarantee nor any impossibility for OBMRR. Thus it provides tools/partial progress but does not solve the stated open problem.

sources.title: Online matching: A brief survey
sources.theorem_or_result:
This paper is a survey and does not prove new competitive-ratio theorems for online bipartite matching with reusable resources. It summarizes known results, including that for OBMRR with identical deterministic usage duration \(d\):
- Fractional/divisible relaxation admits an optimal \(1-1/e\) competitive ratio via generalized balance (citing [GIU21, FNS21]).
- Integral (randomized) algorithms achieve competitive ratios \(0.589\) via periodic reranking and \(0.505\) via an OCS-based algorithm (citing [DFN+23]), extending to vertex-weighted settings.
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether vertex-weighted OBMRR with deterministic usage duration \(d\) admits a randomized online algorithm with competitive ratio \(1-1/e\) against \(\mathrm{OPT}(G,d)\), or else what the optimal ratio is. This survey does not resolve that question; it reports that \(1-1/e\) is known for the fractional version, while the best known guarantees for the integral version (even with identical \(d\) across resources) are below \(1-1/e\) (\(0.589\) and \(0.505\)). It also notes that heterogeneous durations \(d_v\) remain open, but that is a different open direction than achieving \(1-1/e\) for the integral identical-\(d\) model.
